sections.heading: Power regarding summons, production of documents and evidence, etc.
sections.title: Adjudicating Authority powers for evidence collection
sections.summary: The Adjudicating Authority has powers equivalent to a civil court under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. This includes summoning persons, enforcing attendance, compelling production of records, receiving affidavits, issuing commissions, and other related matters. All summoned persons must attend in person or through agents and state the truth.
sections.dtia_summary: This section grants the Adjudicating Authority extensive powers to collect evidence, which may impact data transfers and cross-border investigations involving financial institutions.

requirements.text: Adjudicating Authority has powers equivalent to a civil court under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908
Adjudicating Authority may compel production of records from banking companies and financial institutions
All summoned persons must attend in person or through agents and state the truth

sections.source_quote: 11. Power regarding summons, production of documents and evidence, etc.—(1) The Adjudicating Authority shall, for the purposes of this Act, have the same powers as are vested in a civil court under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (5 of 1908) while trying a suit in respect of the following matters, namely:— (a) discovery and inspection; (b) enforcing the attendance of any person, including any officer of a banking company or a financial institution or a company, and examining him on oath; (c) compelling the production of records; (d) receiving evidence on affidavits; (e) issuing commissions for examination of witnesses and documents; and (f) any other matter which may be prescribed.
sections.relevance_reason: Adjudicating Authority powers for evidence collection

sections.heading: Reporting entity to maintain records.
sections.title: Record-keeping for financial entities
sections.summary: Reporting entities must maintain transaction records for five years, provide information to the Director within prescribed timelines, keep records confidential, and maintain identity records for five years after the business relationship ends. Records must be kept confidential unless otherwise provided by law.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires detailed record-keeping for financial transactions, impacting data retention and sharing across borders for regulatory compliance.

requirements.text: Reporting entities must maintain transaction records for five years from transaction date
Reporting entities must provide transaction information to the Director within prescribed timelines
All information maintained must be kept confidential unless otherwise provided by law
Identity records must be maintained for five years after business relationship ends

sections.source_quote: 12. Reporting entity to maintain records.—(1) Every reporting entity shall— (a) maintain a record of all transactions, including information relating to transactions covered under clause (b), in such manner as to enable it to reconstruct individual transactions; (b) furnish to the Director within such time as may be prescribed, information relating to such transactions, whether attempted or executed, the nature and value of which may be prescribed; (e) maintain record of documents evidencing identity of its clients and beneficial owners as well as account files and business correspondence relating to its clients.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data record-keeping with confidentiality

sections.heading: Retention of records.
sections.title: Retention of seized records and property for adjudication
sections.summary: Records or property seized or frozen under sections 17 or 18 may be retained for up to 180 days if the officer believes they are needed for adjudication. The officer must forward the retention order to the Adjudicating Authority. After 180 days, records must be returned unless the Adjudicating Authority permits extended retention. The person from whom records were seized may obtain copies.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs the retention period for records and property, which could affect data transfer if records are held internationally or require cross-border data sharing.

requirements.text: Records or property seized or frozen may be retained for up to 180 days if the officer believes they are needed for adjudication.
The officer must forward the retention order to the Adjudicating Authority.
After 180 days, records must be returned unless the Adjudicating Authority permits extended retention.
The person from whom records were seized may obtain copies of records.
The Adjudicating Authority must satisfy itself that records are needed for adjudication before extending retention.

sections.source_quote: 21. Retention of records.—(1) Where any records have been seized, under section 17 or section 18 or frozen under sub-section (1A) of section 17 and the Investigating Officer or any other officer authorised by the Director in this behalf has reason to believe that any of such records are required to be retained for any inquiry under this Act, such records may if seized, be retained or if frozen, may continue to remain frozen, for a period not exceeding one hundred and eighty days from the day on which such records were seized or frozen, as the case may be.
sections.relevance_reason: Retention of personal data for adjudication

sections.heading: Presumption as to records or property in certain cases.
sections.title: Presumption of ownership and authenticity for records or property
sections.summary: Records or property found in the possession of a person during a survey or search are presumed to belong to that person. The contents of the records are presumed true, and the signatures are presumed to be in the person's handwriting. This presumption applies to records received from outside India if authenticated by an authority.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes presumptions that could impact data transfer by providing a legal basis for authenticity and ownership of records across borders.

requirements.text: Records or property found in the possession of a person during a survey or search are presumed to belong to that person.
The contents of such records are presumed true.
Signatures on records are presumed to be in the person's handwriting.
Records received from outside India, authenticated by an authority, are presumed to be valid for evidence.

sections.source_quote: 22. Presumption as to records or property in certain cases.—(1) Where any records or property are or is found in the possession or control of any person in the course of a survey or a search it shall be presumed that—
sections.relevance_reason: Presumption of ownership not data protection

sections.heading: Power to remove difficulties.
sections.title: Power to resolve transfer difficulties
sections.summary: The Act grants the authority to resolve difficulties related to the transfer of property or records. This power is used to address challenges that may arise during the implementation of the Act, particularly in cases involving cross-border financial transactions or record transfers.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides a mechanism to resolve transfer issues, which may impact data transfer assessments by allowing flexibility in cross-border data handling scenarios.

requirements.text: Authority to resolve difficulties related to property or record transfers

sections.source_quote: 75. Power to remove difficulties.
sections.relevance_reason: Power to resolve transfer difficulties

sections.heading: Definitions.—(1) In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires,—
sections.title: Cross-border data sharing for financial and narcotics law enforcement
sections.summary: This section authorises officers, authorities, or bodies performing functions under tax, foreign exchange, or narcotics laws to share information received or obtained during their duties. The Central Government may specify additional information categories through official notification for public interest purposes. The information shared must be necessary for the specified functions under relevant laws.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a mechanism for cross-border data sharing between Indian authorities and foreign entities for financial and narcotics law enforcement purposes, requiring explicit notification for information sharing to be valid for international data transfers.

requirements.text: Officers, authorities, or bodies performing functions under tax, foreign exchange, or narcotics laws may share information received or obtained during their duties
Central Government may specify additional information categories through official notification for public interest purposes
Information shared must be necessary for specified functions under relevant laws

sections.title: Appeal time extensions for financial entities
sections.summary: Financial entities may appeal to the Appellate Tribunal after the 45-day period if the Appellate Tribunal finds sufficient cause for delay. The Tribunal must provide an opportunity for hearing before making final orders.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows for time extensions in appeals for financial entities, which could impact data transfer if financial data is involved in cross-border disputes.

sections.heading: The words "and includes a person carrying on designated business or profession" omitted by Act 2 of 2013, s. 2
sections.title: Presumption of authenticity for records received from outside India
sections.summary: Records received from outside India, authenticated by an authority, are presumed to be valid for evidence. The signature and other parts of the records are presumed to be in the correct handwriting, and the records are admitted in evidence even if not properly stamped.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides a legal presumption for records received internationally, which could affect data transfer by establishing authenticity standards for cross-border data.
sections.authority: Special Court, Appellate Tribunal, Adjudicating Authority

requirements.text: Records received from outside India, authenticated by an authority, are presumed to be valid for evidence.
Signatures on records are presumed to be in the correct handwriting.
Records are admitted in evidence even if not properly stamped.

sections.source_quote: 22. Presumption as to records or property in certain cases.—(1) Where any records or property are or is found in the possession or control of any person in the course of a survey or a search it shall be presumed that— (i) such records or property belong or belongs to such person; (ii) the contents of such records are true; and (iii) the signature and every other part of such records which purport to be in the handwriting of any particular person or which may reasonably be assumed to have been signed by, or to be in the handwriting of, any particular person, are in that person's handwriting, and in the case of a record, stamped, executed or attested, that it was executed or attested by the person by whom it purports to have been so stamped, executed or attested.

sections.heading: Adjudication.—(1) On receipt of a complaint under sub-section (5) of section 5, or applications made
sections.title: Adjudication of money laundering properties
sections.summary: The Adjudicating Authority may serve a 30-day notice on individuals suspected of money laundering to disclose income sources, assets, and evidence. If the notice specifies property held jointly, copies must be served to all owners. The Authority then considers replies, hears parties, and records findings on whether property is involved in money laundering. If confirmed, property is confiscated by the Central Government.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes procedures for identifying and confiscating property linked to money laundering, requiring detailed disclosure and judicial review before cross-border asset seizure.

requirements.text: Adjudicating Authority may serve 30-day notice on persons suspected of money laundering to disclose income sources, assets, and evidence
If notice specifies property held jointly, copies must be served to all owners
Adjudicating Authority must consider replies, hear parties, and record findings on whether property is involved in money laundering
If property is confirmed involved in money laundering, Adjudicating Authority must confirm attachment or confiscation by Central Government

sections.source_quote: 8. Adjudication.—(1) On receipt of a complaint under sub-section (5) of section 5, or applications made under sub-section (4) of section 17 or under sub-section (10) of section 18, if the Adjudicating Authority has reason to believe that any person has committed an offence under section 3 or is in possession of proceeds of crime, it may serve a notice of not less than thirty days on such person calling upon him to indicate the sources of his income, earning or assets, out of which or by means of which he has acquired the property attached under sub-section (1) of section 5, or, seized or frozen under section 17 or section 18, the evidence on which he relies and other relevant information and particulars, and to show cause why all or any of such properties should not be declared to be the properties involved in money-laundering and confiscated by the Central Government
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data, cross-border transfers, confiscation

sections.title: International maritime and company offences
sections.summary: This section outlines offences related to maritime navigation, company fraud, customs violations, and cross-border implications. It specifies penalties for false declarations, offences against property, and wilful attempts to evade taxes under the Black Money Act.

sections.heading: Power of survey.—(1) Notwithstanding anything contained in any other provisions of this Act,
sections.title: Power to conduct surveys for money laundering offences
sections.summary: An authority may enter places within their assigned area or areas authorized by another authority to inspect records, check crime proceeds, and gather information relevant to money laundering offences. The authority must record reasons for belief in writing and forward these to the Adjudicating Authority within a sealed envelope after completing the survey. The authority may also make copies, inventories, and record statements of persons present.

requirements.text: An authority may enter any place within their assigned area or areas authorized by another authority
The authority must record reasons for belief in writing
The authority must forward reasons and material to Adjudicating Authority in sealed envelope
The authority may make extracts or copies of records
The authority may make an inventory of property checked
The authority may record statements of persons present

sections.source_quote: 16. sections.title: Authority to search and seize property for money laundering
sections.summary: An officer authorized by the Central Government may search buildings, vessels, vehicles, or aircraft where money laundering records or proceeds are suspected. The officer may break locks, seize records or property, mark items, make copies, and examine persons on oath. The officer must forward reasons for belief to the Adjudicating Authority after search and seizure.

The officer may examine persons on oath relevant to investigations
The officer must forward reasons for belief to Adjudicating Authority after search

sections.heading: Search of persons.—(1) If an authority, authorised in this behalf by the Central Government by
sections.title: Search and seizure of persons for money laundering
sections.summary: An authority authorized by the Central Government may search persons suspected of possessing money laundering records or proceeds. The authority must record reasons for belief in writing, forward these to the Adjudicating Authority, and take persons to a Gazetted Officer or Magistrate within 24 hours if requested. The search must be witnessed by two or more persons, and a list of seized items must be signed by witnesses.

The authority must record reasons for belief in writing
The authority must forward reasons and material to Adjudicating Authority

The search must be witnessed by two or more persons
The authority must prepare a list of seized items with witness signatures

sections.source_quote: 18. sections.heading: Power to arrest.—(1) If the Director, Deputy Director, Assistant Director or any other officer
sections.title: Arrest authority and reporting requirements for suspects
sections.summary: The Director, Deputy Director, Assistant Director, or authorized officer may arrest a person suspected of an offence under this Act if they have reason to believe it based on material in their possession. The officer must record the reason for arrest in writing and inform the person of the grounds. Within 24 hours, the arrested person must be taken before a Magistrate. The officer must forward a copy of the arrest order and related material to the Adjudicating Authority within 24 hours.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a process for arrests and reporting to the Adjudicating Authority, which may impact data transfer if arrests involve cross-border individuals or data collection from foreign sources.

requirements.text: The Director, Deputy Director, Assistant Director or authorized officer may arrest a person suspected of an offence under this Act if they have reason to believe it based on material in their possession.
The officer must record the reason for arrest in writing.
The officer must inform the person of the grounds for arrest.
The arrested person must be taken before a Magistrate within 24 hours.
The officer must forward a copy of the arrest order and related material to the Adjudicating Authority within 24 hours.

sections.source_quote: 19. Power to arrest.—(1) If the Director, Deputy Director, Assistant Director or any other officer authorised in this behalf by the Central Government by general or special order, has on the basis of material in his possession, reason to believe (the reason for such belief to be recorded in writing) that any person has been guilty of an offence punishable under this Act, he may arrest such person and shall, as soon as may be, inform him of the grounds for such arrest.
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.authority: Special Court, Appellate Tribunal, Adjudicating Authority

requirements.text: Records received from outside India, authenticated by an authority, are presumed to be valid for evidence.
Signatures on records are presumed to be in the correct handwriting.
Records are admitted in evidence even if not properly stamped.

sections.source_quote: 22. Presumption as to records or property in certain cases.—(1) Where any records or property are or is found in the possession or control of any person in the course of a survey or a search it shall be presumed that— (i) such records or property belong or belongs to such person; (ii) the contents of such records are true; and (iii) the signature and every other part of such records which purport to be in the handwriting of any particular person or which may reasonably be assumed to have been signed by, or to be in the handwriting of, any particular person, are in that person's handwriting, and in the case of a record, stamped, executed or attested, that it was executed or attested by the person by whom it purports to have been so stamped, executed or attested.
sections.relevance_reason: Not relevant to data compliance

sections.heading: Appeal to Appellate Tribunal.—(1) Save as otherwise provided in sub-section (3), the Director
sections.title: Appeal process to Appellate Tribunal
sections.summary: The Director or any person aggrieved by an order from the Adjudicating Authority may appeal to the Appellate Tribunal within 45 days. The Appellate Tribunal may entertain appeals filed after the 45-day period if it finds sufficient cause for delay. The Tribunal must pass orders confirming, modifying, or setting aside the appealed order.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines the appeal process, which could impact data transfer if appeals involve cross-border data disputes or international jurisdiction.

requirements.text: The Director or any person aggrieved by an order from the Adjudicating Authority may appeal to the Appellate Tribunal.
Appeals must be filed within 45 days of receiving a copy of the order.
The Appellate Tribunal may entertain appeals filed after the 45-day period if it finds sufficient cause for delay.
The Appellate Tribunal must pass orders confirming, modifying, or setting aside the appealed order.

sections.source_quote: 26. Appeal to Appellate Tribunal.—(1) Save as otherwise provided in sub-section (3), the Director or any person aggrieved by an order made by the Adjudicating Authority under this Act, may prefer an appeal to the Appellate Tribunal.
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: Subs. by Act 2 of 2013, s. 20, for "banking company, financial institution or intermediary" (w.e.f. 15-2-2013).
sections.title: Appeal time extensions for financial entities
sections.summary: Financial entities may appeal to the Appellate Tribunal after the 45-day period if the Appellate Tribunal finds sufficient cause for delay. The Tribunal must provide an opportunity for hearing before making final orders.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows for time extensions in appeals for financial entities, which could impact data transfer if financial data is involved in cross-border disputes.

requirements.text: Financial entities may appeal to the Appellate Tribunal after the 45-day period if the Appellate Tribunal finds sufficient cause for delay.
The Appellate Tribunal must provide an opportunity for hearing before making final orders.

sections.heading: Offences triable by Special Courts.—(1) Notwithstanding anything contained in the Code of
sections.title: Offences triable by Special Courts for money laundering
sections.summary: This section specifies that certain money laundering offences and connected scheduled offences are triable by Special Courts. It mandates that Special Courts continue trying scheduled offences from before this Act's commencement. It also outlines procedures for taking cognizance of offences without committing the accused, and requires closure reports if no money laundering offence is found. The Special Court must proceed with trials following the Code of Criminal Procedure.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes jurisdictional rules for Special Courts handling money laundering cases, which could impact cross-border data transfers if the Special Court's jurisdiction extends to foreign entities or data involved in such cases.

requirements.text: Scheduled offences connected to money laundering offences are triable by Special Courts
Special Courts continue trying scheduled offences from before this Act's commencement
Special Court may take cognizance of money laundering offences without committing accused
After investigation, if no money laundering offence is found, authority must submit closure report
Special Court must proceed with trials following Code of Criminal Procedure

sections.source_quote: Offences triable by Special Courts.—(1) Notwithstanding anything contained in the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (2 of 1974),—
sections.relevance_reason: Money laundering courts and financial data handling

sections.heading: Powers of authorities regarding summons, production of documents and to give evidence,
sections.title: Powers for summons and document production in financial investigations
sections.summary: This section grants the Director powers equivalent to a civil court under the Code of Civil Procedure for summoning persons, examining witnesses, compelling document production, and receiving affidavits during financial investigations. It specifically includes reporting entities and financial institutions as subjects of these powers.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the legal authority for financial investigations, which could affect data transfer processes if investigations require cross-border data collection or sharing.

requirements.text: Director has powers to compel attendance of persons including reporting entities
Director may examine persons on oath
Director may compel production of records
Director may receive evidence on affidavits

sections.source_quote: The Director shall, for the purposes of section 13, have the same powers as are vested in a civil court under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (5 of 1908) while trying a suit
sections.relevance_reason: Director's powers for document production in financial investigations

sections.heading: Subs. by Act 2 of 2013, s. 22, for "banking company or a financial institution or a company," (w.e.f. 15-2-2013).
sections.title: Financial and narcotics-related offences
sections.summary: This section lists specific offences related to financial crimes, narcotics, explosives, unlawful activities, terrorism, and wildlife protection. It includes descriptions of crimes such as dishonestly preventing debt from creditors, forgery of valuable securities, and contraventions of narcotics laws. The offences are categorized under various acts including the Indian Penal Code and other relevant legislation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section describes specific offences that may impact data handling practices for financial and narcotics-related activities, but does not directly address data transfer mechanisms.

sections.title: Cross-border data sharing for financial and narcotics law enforcement
sections.summary: This section authorises officers, authorities, or bodies performing functions under tax, foreign exchange, or narcotics laws to share information received or obtained during their duties. The Central Government may specify additional information categories through official notification for public interest purposes. The information shared must be necessary for the specified functions under relevant laws.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a mechanism for cross-border data sharing between Indian authorities and foreign entities for financial and narcotics law enforcement purposes, requiring explicit notification for information sharing to be valid for international data transfers.

sections.title: Definitions for cross-border financial arrangements
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in cross-border financial arrangements, including 'contracting State' which refers to countries outside India with which arrangements have been made by the Central Government through treaties or other means.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides definitions critical for understanding cross-border financial arrangements, which directly impacts data transfer impact assessments involving international entities.

requirements.text: Contracting State means any country or place outside India with arrangements made by Central Government

sections.heading: Letter of request to a contracting State in certain cases.—(1) Notwithstanding anything
sections.title: Cross-border evidence requests for investigations
sections.summary: This section permits Special Courts in India to issue letters of request to courts in contracting States when evidence is needed for investigations under the Act. The request must specify that evidence may be available in a foreign jurisdiction and must include instructions for examining facts, taking specified steps, and forwarding collected evidence. The request is transmitted per Central Government specifications and evidence obtained is deemed part of the Indian investigation.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows cross-border evidence collection via formal requests; relevant for DTIAs involving international evidence gathering procedures.

requirements.text: Special Court may issue letter of request to foreign court for examination of facts and circumstances
Special Court may specify steps to take in the foreign jurisdiction
Foreign court must forward all evidence collected to Special Court

sections.source_quote: 57. Letter of request to a contracting State in certain cases.—(1) Notwithstanding anything contained in this Act or the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (2 of 1974) if, in the course of an investigation into an offence or other proceedings under this Act, an application is made to a Special Court by the Investigating Officer or any officer superior in rank to the Investigating Officer that any evidence is required in connection with investigation into an offence or proceedings under this Act and he is of the opinion that such evidence may be available in any place in a contracting State, and the Special Court, on being satisfied that such evidence is required in connection with the investigation into an offence or proceedings under this Act, may issue a letter of request to a court or an authority in the contracting State competent to deal with such request to—
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border evidence requests for investigations

sections.heading: Reciprocal arrangements for processes and assistance for transfer of accused persons.—(1)
sections.title: Cross-border enforcement of legal orders
sections.summary: This section enables Indian Special Courts to execute legal orders (summons, warrants, etc.) in contracting States and vice versa. It requires sending duplicate orders to foreign courts through Central Government-specified authorities, and allows foreign-issued orders to be treated as valid within Indian jurisdiction. It also mandates forwarding of executed documents to the originating court and imposes conditions on transferred prisoners.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows cross-border enforcement of legal orders; relevant for DTIAs involving international legal cooperation and prisoner transfers.

requirements.text: Special Court must send summons or warrants in duplicate to foreign courts through Central Government-specified authorities
Foreign court must execute orders as if issued by another Indian court
Executed warrants must follow procedures in section 19 for arrests
Executed search warrants must follow procedures in sections 17 and 18 for seized items
Documents or items from executed warrants must be forwarded to originating court
Transferred prisoners may have conditions imposed by Indian court or government

sections.source_quote: 59. Reciprocal arrangements for processes and assistance for transfer of accused persons.—(1) Where a Special Court, in relation to an offence punishable under section 4, desires that— (a) a summons to an accused person, or (b) a warrant for the arrest of an accused person, or (c) a summons to any person requiring him to attend and produce a document or other thing or to produce it, or (d) a search warrant, issued by it shall be served or executed at any place in any contracting State, it shall send such summons or warrant in duplicate in such form, to such Court, Judge or Magistrate through such authorities, as the Central Government may, by notification, specify in this behalf
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border enforcement of legal orders

sections.heading: Attachment, seizure and confiscation, etc., of property in a contracting State or India.—(1)
sections.title: Cross-border property enforcement
sections.summary: This section governs cross-border enforcement of property orders. It allows Indian courts to request foreign courts to execute property attachment, seizure, freezing, or confiscation orders. It also enables foreign courts to request Indian authorities to enforce property orders related to offences committed in foreign jurisdictions. The process includes tracing property, conducting investigations, and handling confiscated assets with options for return or compensation to the requesting state.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides mechanisms for cross-border enforcement of property orders; relevant for DTIAs involving international asset tracing and confiscation.

requirements.text: Special Court may issue letter of request to foreign court for execution of property orders
Central Government may forward foreign requests for property enforcement to Director
Director must direct authorities to trace and identify property
Tracing steps may include inquiries, investigations, or surveys of persons, places, properties, assets, documents, books of account
Inquiries must be conducted by authorities under this Act
Confiscated property may be returned to requesting state or compensated by disposal on mutually agreed terms

sections.source_quote: 60. Attachment, seizure and confiscation, etc., of property in a contracting State or India.—(1) Where the Director has made an order for attachment of any [property under section 5 or for freezing under sub-section (1A) of section 17 or where an Adjudicating Authority has made an order relating to a property under section 8 or where a Special Court has made an order of confiscation relating to a property under sub-section (5) or sub-section (6) of section 8] and such property is suspected to be in a contracting State, the Special Court, on an application by the Director or the Administrator appointed under sub-section (1) of section 10, as the case may be , may issue a letter of request to a court or an authority in the contracting State for execution of such order.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border enforcement of financial assets

sections.heading: Punishment for vexatious search.—Any authority or officer exercising powers under this Act or
sections.title: Penalties for unlawful searches
sections.summary: This section imposes penalties for authorities or officers who conduct searches without written reasons. It covers unlawful searches of buildings/places and unlawful detention or arrest of persons. Offenders face imprisonment up to two years or a fine up to 50,000 rupees or both.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets penalties for unlawful searches; relevant for DTIAs involving security and privacy safeguards.

requirements.text: Any authority or officer exercising powers under this Act must have written reasons before searching buildings or places
Any authority or officer exercising powers under this Act must have written reasons before detaining or arresting persons

sections.source_quote: 62. Punishment for vexatious search.—Any authority or officer exercising powers under this Act or any rules made thereunder, who, without reasons recorded in writing,— (a) searches or causes to be searched any building or place; or (b) detains or searches or arrests any person, shall for every such offence be liable on conviction for imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years or fine which may extend to fifty thousand rupees or both.
sections.relevance_reason: Unlawful searches not data protection specific

sections.heading: Punishment for false information or failure to give information, etc.—(I) Any person wilfully
sections.title: Penalties for false information or non-compliance
sections.summary: This section imposes penalties for false information leading to arrests or searches, refusal to answer questions, refusal to sign statements, or failure to attend as required by summons. Offenders face fines up to 10,000 rupees per default or imprisonment up to two years. It also requires providing an opportunity to be heard before imposing penalties.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets penalties for non-compliance with legal procedures; relevant for DTIAs involving data subject rights and compliance.

requirements.text: Any person wilfully giving false information causing arrest or search must face imprisonment up to two years or fine up to 50,000 rupees
Persons refusing to answer questions, sign statements, or attend summons face fines up to 10,000 rupees per default
Authority must provide opportunity to be heard before imposing penalties

sections.source_quote: 63. Punishment for false information or failure to give information, etc.—(I) Any person wilfully and maliciously giving false information and so causing an arrest or a search to be made under this Act shall on conviction be liable for imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years or with fine which may extend to fifty thousand rupees or both.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: general procedure for compliance

sections.heading: Power to make rules.—(1) The Central Government may, by notification, make rules for
sections.title: Rules for financial intelligence and record-keeping
sections.summary: This section grants the Central Government authority to make rules for implementing the Act, including specific provisions for record-keeping formats, property attachment procedures, salary structures for adjudicating authorities, and methods for managing confiscated property. The rules may also specify authentication procedures for client identities and transaction monitoring.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes detailed record-keeping and authentication requirements for financial intelligence operations, impacting data storage and sharing practices across borders for AML/CFT purposes.
